questions.text: Object-oriented programming allows you to derive new classes from existing classes. This is called ____________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: encapsulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: inheritance

choices.label: C
choices.text: abstraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: generalization

questions.explanation: Inheritance is the core concept in object-oriented programming that allows a new class (subclass) to be derived from an existing class (superclass), inheriting its properties and behaviors.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: A subclass is a subset of a superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subclass is usually extended to contain more functions and more detailed information than its superclass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: "class A extends B" means A is a subclass of B.

choices.label: D
choices.text: "class A extends B" means B is a subclass of A.

questions.explanation: In Java, inheritance is defined using the 'extends' keyword where 'class A extends B' identifies A as the subclass. While a subclass is a 'subtype' of the superclass, it is usually broader in functionality (a superset of features) because it adds specific fields and methods to the ones inherited.

questions.text: Inheritance means ______________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: that data fields should be declared private

choices.label: B
choices.text: that a class can extend another class

choices.label: C
choices.text: that a variable of supertype can refer to a subtype object

choices.label: D
choices.text: that a class can contain another class

questions.explanation: Inheritance is the mechanism by which one class acquires the properties (methods and fields) of another. In Java, this relationship is established when one class extends another.

questions.text:
Suppose you create a class Square to be a subclass of GeometricObject. Analyze the following code:

class Square extends GeometricObject {
  double length;
  
  Square(double length) {
    GeometricObject(length);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program compiles fine, but you cannot create an instance of Square because the constructor does not specify the length of the Square.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error because you attempted to invoke the GeometricObject class's constructor illegally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program compiles fine, but it has a runtime error because of invoking the Square class's constructor illegally.

questions.explanation: To call a superclass constructor in Java, you must use the 'super' keyword (e.g., super(length)). Directly calling the class name as a method inside a constructor is syntactically incorrect and causes a compile error.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code. Please select all that apply.

public class A extends B {
}

class B {
  public B(String s) {
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error because A does not have a default constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error because the default constructor of A invokes the default constructor of B, but B does not have a default constructor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program would compile fine if you add the following constructor into A: A(String s) { }

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program would compile fine if you add the following constructor into A: A(String s) { super(s); }

questions.explanation: Because class B defines a constructor with a parameter, the compiler does not provide a default no-arg constructor. Class A's implicit default constructor tries to call 'super()', which doesn't exist in B, causing an error. This is fixed if A provides a constructor that explicitly calls 'super(s)'.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test extends A { 
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Test t = new Test();
    t.print();
  }
}

class A {
  String s;

  A(String s) {
    this.s = s;
  }

  public void print() {
    System.out.println(s);
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program does not compile because Test does not have a default constructor Test().

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has an implicit default constructor Test(), but it cannot be compiled, because its super class does not have a default constructor. The program would compile if the constructor in the class A were removed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program would compile if a default constructor A(){ } is added to class A explicitly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program compiles, but it has a runtime error due to the conflict on the method name print.

questions.explanation: A subclass constructor automatically calls the no-arg constructor of its superclass. Since class A defines a constructor with parameters, it loses its default no-arg constructor. Therefore, the default constructor of Test fails unless a no-arg constructor is added to A or the existing constructor in A is removed.

questions.text:
What is the output of running class C?

class A {
  public A() {
    System.out.println(
      "The default constructor of A is invoked");
  }
}

class B extends A {
  public B() {
    System.out.println(
      "The default constructor of B is invoked");
  }
}

public class C  {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    B b = new B();
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nothing displayed

choices.label: B
choices.text: "The default constructor of B is invoked"

choices.label: C
choices.text: "The default constructor of A is invoked" followed by "The default constructor of B is invoked"

choices.label: D
choices.text: "The default constructor of B is invoked" followed by "The default constructor of A is invoked"

choices.label: E
choices.text: "The default constructor of A is invoked"

questions.explanation: Java follows a process called constructor chaining. When an instance of a subclass (B) is created, its constructor first invokes the constructor of the superclass (A). Thus, the output shows A's constructor message followed by B's constructor message.

questions.text: Which of the following is incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A constructor may be static.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A constructor may be private.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A constructor may invoke a static method.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A constructor may invoke an overloaded constructor.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A constructor invokes its superclass no-arg constructor by default if a constructor does not invoke an overloaded constructor or its superclass's constructor.

questions.explanation: Constructors are meant to initialize instance-specific data and are tied to the creation of an object instance, so they cannot be declared as static.

questions.text: Which of the statements regarding the super keyword is incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: You can use super to invoke a super class constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You can use super to invoke a super class method.

choices.label: C
choices.text: You can use super.super.p to invoke a method in superclass's parent class.

choices.label: D
choices.text: You cannot invoke a method in superclass's parent class.

questions.explanation: The 'super' keyword can only be used to access members of the immediate superclass. Syntax like 'super.super' is not allowed in Java to bypass the parent class and reach the grandparent class.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test { 
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    B b = new B();
    b.m(5);
    System.out.println("i is " + b.i);
  }
}

class A {
  int i;

  public void m(int i) {
    this.i = i;
  }
}

class B extends A {
  public void m(String s) {
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program has a compile error, because m is overridden with a different signature in B.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a compile error, because b.m(5) cannot be invoked since the method m(int) is hidden in B.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has a runtime error on b.i, because i is not accessible from b.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The method m is not overridden in B. B inherits the method m from A and defines an overloaded method m in B.

questions.explanation: Method overriding requires the signature (name and parameters) to be identical. Since class B defines m(String) while inheriting m(int) from A, the method m is overloaded, not overridden, meaning both versions are available to instances of class B.

questions.text:
The getValue() method is overridden in two ways. Which one is correct?

I:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A a = new A();
    System.out.println(a.getValue());
  }
}

class B {
  public String getValue() {
    return "Any object";
  }
}

class A extends B {
  public Object getValue() {
    return "A string";
  }
}

II:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A a = new A();
    System.out.println(a.getValue());
  }
}

class B {
  public Object getValue() {
    return "Any object";
  }
}

class A extends B {
  public String getValue() {
    return "A string";
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: I

choices.label: B
choices.text: II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both I and II

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither

questions.explanation: In Java, an overriding method in a subclass must have a return type that is the same as, or a subtype of (covariant return type), the return type declared in the superclass. In code II, class A's getValue returns String, which is a subtype of Object (from class B), making it valid. In code I, class A's getValue returns Object, which is a supertype of String (from class B), which is not allowed.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: To override a method, the method must be defined in the subclass using the same signature and compatible return type as in its superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Overloading a method is to provide more than one method with the same name but with different signatures to distinguish them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a compile error if two methods differ only in return type in the same class.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A private method cannot be overridden. If a method defined in a subclass is private in its superclass, the two methods are completely unrelated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A static method cannot be overridden. If a static method defined in the superclass is redefined in a subclass, the method defined in the superclass is hidden.

questions.explanation: These statements correctly define the fundamental rules of Java inheritance: A and D describe overriding (same signature, compatible return, non-private), B and C describe overloading (same name, different signature), and E explains that static methods are hidden rather than overridden.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    new B();
  }
}

class A {
  int i = 7;
  
  public A() {
    System.out.println("i from A is " + i);
  }
  
  public void setI(int i) {
    this.i = 2 * i;
  }
}

class B extends A {
  public B() {
    setI(20);
    // System.out.println("i from B is " + i);
  }
  
  @Override
  public void setI(int i) {
    this.i = 3 * i;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The constructor of class A is not called.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 7".

choices.label: C
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 40".

choices.label: D
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 60".

questions.explanation: When 'new B()' is called, the constructor of its superclass A is executed first. In the code provided, A's constructor prints the value of 'i' immediately. At this point, 'i' is 7. The call to 'setI(20)' occurs in class B's constructor, which happens after class A's constructor has already finished its execution and print statement.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    new B();
  }
}

class A {
  int i = 7;
  
  public A() {
    setI(20);
    System.out.println("i from A is " + i);
  }
  
  public void setI(int i) {
    this.i = 2 * i;
  }
}

class B extends A {
  public B() {
    // System.out.println("i from B is " + i);
  }
  
  @Override
  public void setI(int i) {
    this.i = 3 * i;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The constructor of class A is not called.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 7".

choices.label: C
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 40".

choices.label: D
choices.text: The constructor of class A is called and it displays "i from A is 60".

questions.explanation: Because of dynamic binding, when class A's constructor calls 'setI(20)', it invokes the version of the method overridden in class B. Class B's 'setI' method sets 'i' to 3 * 20 (60). Consequently, the print statement in class A's constructor displays 'i from A is 60'.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: A method can be overloaded in the same class.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A method can be overridden in the same class.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a method overloads another method, these two methods must have the same signature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a method overrides another method, these two methods must have the same signature.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A method in a subclass can overload a method in the superclass.

questions.explanation: Overloading refers to methods with the same name but different signatures (same or different classes), while overriding refers to a subclass providing a specific implementation for a method with the same signature already defined in its superclass.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code. Please select all that apply.

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object a1 = new A();
    Object a2 = new Object();
    System.out.println(a1);
    System.out.println(a2);
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  @Override
  public String toString() {
    return "A's x is " + x;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program cannot be compiled, because System.out.println(a1) is wrong and it should be replaced by System.out.println(a1.toString());

choices.label: B
choices.text: When executing System.out.println(a1), the toString() method in the Object class is invoked.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When executing System.out.println(a2), the toString() method in the Object class is invoked.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When executing System.out.println(a1), the toString() method in the A class is invoked.

questions.explanation: System.out.println(object) implicitly calls object.toString(). Through polymorphism and dynamic binding, the JVM identifies the actual type of the object at runtime: 'a1' refers to an instance of class A, so A's toString() is called; 'a2' refers to an instance of Object, so the default Object.toString() is called.

questions.text:
Given the following code, find the compile error. Please select all that apply.

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    m(new GraduateStudent());
    m(new Student());
    m(new Person());
    m(new Object());
  }

  public static void m(Student x) {
    System.out.println(x.toString());
  }
}

class GraduateStudent extends Student {
}

class Student extends Person {
  @Override
  public String toString() {
    return "Student";
  }
}

class Person extends Object {
  @Override
  public String toString() {
    return "Person";
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: m(new GraduateStudent()) causes an error

choices.label: B
choices.text: m(new Student()) causes an error

choices.label: C
choices.text: m(new Person()) causes an error

choices.label: D
choices.text: m(new Object()) causes an error

questions.explanation: The method 'm' is defined to accept a parameter of type 'Student'. Because of inheritance, it can also accept any subclass of Student (like GraduateStudent). However, it cannot accept superclasses like 'Person' or 'Object' because a Person is not necessarily a Student.

questions.text: Polymorphism means ______________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: that data fields should be declared private

choices.label: B
choices.text: that a class can extend another class

choices.label: C
choices.text: that a variable of supertype can refer to a subtype object

choices.label: D
choices.text: that a class can contain another class

questions.explanation: Polymorphism is the capability of a variable of a supertype to refer to objects of any of its subtypes. This allows a single reference type to behave differently depending on the specific instance it points to at runtime.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    new Person().printPerson();
    new Student().printPerson();
  }
}

class Student extends Person {
  @Override
  public String getInfo() {
    return "Student";
  }
}

class Person {
  public String getInfo() {
    return "Person";
  }
  
  public void printPerson() {
    System.out.println(getInfo());
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Person Person

choices.label: B
choices.text: Person Student

choices.label: C
choices.text: Student Student

choices.label: D
choices.text: Student Person

questions.explanation: Class Student overrides the 'getInfo' method of class Person. When 'new Student().printPerson()' is called, it executes the 'printPerson' method inherited from Person. This method calls 'getInfo()', and due to dynamic binding, it uses the version defined in the actual object's class (Student).

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    new Person().printPerson();
    new Student().printPerson();
  }
}

class Student extends Person {
  private String getInfo() {
    return "Student";
  }
}

class Person {
  private String getInfo() {
    return "Person";
  }
  
  public void printPerson() {
    System.out.println(getInfo());
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Person Person

choices.label: B
choices.text: Person Student

choices.label: C
choices.text: Student Student

choices.label: D
choices.text: Student Person

questions.explanation: Private methods are not inherited and are not subject to dynamic binding. Since 'getInfo' is private in class Person, the call inside 'printPerson' is bound at compile-time to the private method in class Person. Subclassing does not change this behavior, so both instances call the version in Person.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: You can always pass an instance of a subclass to a parameter of its superclass type. This feature is known as polymorphism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The compiler finds a matching method according to parameter type, number of parameters, and order of the parameters at compile time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A method may be implemented in several subclasses. The Java Virtual Machine dynamically binds the implementation of the method at runtime.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dynamic binding can apply to static methods.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dynamic binding can apply to instance methods.

questions.explanation: Statement D is false because dynamic binding applies only to instance methods, which are resolved at runtime based on the actual object type. Static methods are bound at compile time based on the declared reference type, a process known as static binding.

questions.text: Which of the following are Java keywords?

choices.label: A
choices.text: instanceOf

choices.label: B
choices.text: instanceof

choices.label: C
choices.text: cast

choices.label: D
choices.text: casting

questions.explanation: In Java, 'instanceof' is a reserved keyword used for testing if an object is an instance of a specific class or interface. Java keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in lowercase; 'instanceOf', 'cast', and 'casting' are not valid keywords.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    String s = new String("Welcome to Java");
    Object o = s;
    String d = (String)o;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: When assigning s to o in Object o = s, a new object is created.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When casting o in String d = (String)o, a new object is created.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When casting o in String d = (String)o, the contents of o is changed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: s, o, and d reference the same String object.

questions.explanation: Assigning one reference variable to another (o = s) or casting a reference (d = (String)o) only copies the memory address (reference) of the object. It does not create a new object or modify the existing object's contents; thus, s, o, and d all point to the same instance.

questions.text:
Assume Cylinder is a subtype of Circle. Analyze the following code:

Circle c = new Circle (5);
Cylinder c = cy;

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code has a compile error.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code has a runtime error.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code is fine.

questions.explanation: The code results in a compile error for two reasons: first, the variable 'c' is declared twice in the same scope; second, the variable 'cy' is used in the assignment but has not been defined.

questions.text:
Given the following classes and their objects:

class C1 {};
class C2 extends C1 {};
class C3 extends C1 {};

C2 c2 = new C2();
C3 c3 = new C3();

Analyze the following statement:

c2 = (C2)((C1)c3);

choices.label: A
choices.text: c3 is cast into c2 successfully.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You will get a runtime error because you cannot cast objects from sibling classes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: You will get a runtime error because the Java runtime system cannot perform multiple casting in nested form.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The statement is correct.

questions.explanation: While the syntax allows the cast to C1, the attempt to cast a C3 object into a C2 reference at runtime will trigger a ClassCastException. In Java, you cannot cast an object to a sibling class type (a class it does not inherit from), even if they share a common parent.

questions.text:
Given the following code, which of the following expressions evaluates to false?

class C1 {}
class C2 extends C1 { }
class C3 extends C2 { }
class C4 extends C1 {}

C1 c1 = new C1();
C2 c2 = new C2();
C3 c3 = new C3();
C4 c4 = new C4();

choices.label: A
choices.text: c1 instanceof C1

choices.label: B
choices.text: c2 instanceof C1

choices.label: C
choices.text: c3 instanceof C1

choices.label: D
choices.text: c4 instanceof C2

questions.explanation: The expression 'c4 instanceof C2' is false. Although both C4 and C2 share the parent class C1, there is no inheritance relationship between C4 and C2. An object is only an instance of its own class or any of its superclasses/interfaces.

questions.text: You can assign _________ to a variable of Object[] type. Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: new char[100]

choices.label: B
choices.text: new int[100]

choices.label: C
choices.text: new double[100]

choices.label: D
choices.text: new String[100]

choices.label: E
choices.text: new java.util.Date[100]

questions.explanation: In Java, arrays of reference types are covariant, meaning an array of a subclass (like String[] or Date[]) can be assigned to a variable of type Object[]. However, arrays of primitive types (like int[], char[], or double[]) are objects themselves but are not considered subtypes of Object[].

questions.text:
Assume Cylinder is a subtype of Circle. Analyze the following code:

Cylinder cy = new Cylinder(1, 1);
Circle c = cy;

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code has a compile error.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code has a runtime error.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code is fine.

questions.explanation: The code is correct because it demonstrates 'upcasting'. Since Cylinder is a subtype of Circle, a Cylinder object can be assigned to a reference variable of type Circle without explicit casting.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

// Program 1:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object a1 = new A();
    Object a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(a1.equals(a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(Object a) {
    return this.x == ((A)a).x;
  }
}


// Program 2:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object a1 = new A();
    Object a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(a1.equals(a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(A a) {
    return this.x == a.x;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays false

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays false

questions.explanation: In Program 1, class A overrides 'equals(Object)', so 'a1.equals(a2)' invokes the custom logic (returning true). In Program 2, class A defines 'equals(A)', which overloads rather than overrides the 'equals(Object)' method. Since 'a1' is declared as an Object, the compiler selects the 'Object.equals' method, which checks for reference equality and returns false.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

// Program 1:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object a1 = new A();
    Object a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(a1.equals(a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(A a) {
    return this.x == a.x;
  }
}


// Program 2:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A a1 = new A();
    A a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(a1.equals(a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(A a) {
    return this.x == a.x;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays false

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays false

questions.explanation: In Program 1, 'a1' is declared as an Object, so 'a1.equals(a2)' calls the default Object equals method (checking reference identity), which returns false. In Program 2, 'a1' is declared as type A, allowing the compiler to match the call to the overloaded 'equals(A)' method defined in class A, which returns true.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code.

// Program 1
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object a1 = new A();
    Object a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(((A)a1).equals((A)a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(A a) {
    return this.x == a.x;
  }
}


// Program 2
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A a1 = new A();
    A a2 = new A();
    System.out.println(a1.equals(a2));
  }
}

class A {
  int x;

  public boolean equals(A a) {
    return this.x == a.x;
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays true

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program 1 displays true and Program 2 displays false

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program 1 displays false and Program 2 displays false

questions.explanation: In Program 1, Class A defines 'equals(A a)', which overloads rather than overrides 'Object.equals(Object)'. However, because a1 and a2 are cast to 'A', the compiler invokes the overloaded version in Class A. Since both objects have the default x value of 0, it returns true. In Program 2, the variables are of type 'A', so the compiler again selects the 'equals(A a)' method, returning true.

questions.text: The equals method is defined in the Object class. Which of the following is correct to override it in the String class?

choices.label: A
choices.text: public boolean equals(String other)

choices.label: B
choices.text: public boolean equals(Object other)

choices.label: C
choices.text: public static boolean equals(String other)

choices.label: D
choices.text: public static boolean equals(Object other)

questions.explanation: To override a method in Java, the method signature must match exactly. The 'equals' method in the Object class is defined as 'public boolean equals(Object obj)'. Therefore, any class attempting to override it must use 'Object' as the parameter type. Choice A is an overload, not an override.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Override the equals(Object) method in the Object class whenever possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Override the toString() method in the Object class whenever possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A public default no-arg constructor is assumed if no constructors are defined explicitly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: You should follow standard Java programming style and naming conventions. Choose informative names for classes, data fields, and methods.

questions.explanation: All statements are correct. A: Overriding 'equals' allows for logical equality checks. B: Overriding 'toString' provides a useful string representation of an object. C: Java automatically provides a public no-arg constructor if no other constructors are defined. D: Following naming conventions and using informative names is a fundamental best practice in Java programming.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object o1 = new Object();
    Object o2 = new Object();
    System.out.print((o1 == o2) + " " + (o1.equals(o2)));
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: false false

choices.label: B
choices.text: true true

choices.label: C
choices.text: false true

choices.label: D
choices.text: true false

questions.explanation: The '==' operator compares the memory addresses (references) of the two objects; since 'o1' and 'o2' are two distinct instances created with 'new', they have different addresses, resulting in 'false'. The 'equals' method in the 'Object' class is implemented to use '==', so it also returns 'false' for two different instances.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    String s1 = new String("Java");
    String s2 = new String("Java");
    System.out.print((s1 == s2) + " " + (s1.equals(s2)));
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: false false

choices.label: B
choices.text: true true

choices.label: C
choices.text: false true

choices.label: D
choices.text: true false

questions.explanation: Using 'new String("Java")' explicitly creates two separate objects in memory with different references, so 's1 == s2' is 'false'. However, the 'String' class overrides the 'equals' method to compare the actual character content of the strings, and since both contain 'Java', 's1.equals(s2)' is 'true'.

questions.text: Given two reference variables t1 and t2, if t1 == t2 is true, t1.equals(t2) must be ___________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: true

choices.label: B
choices.text: false

questions.explanation: The 'equals' method contract in Java requires that the method be reflexive, meaning 'x.equals(x)' must always be true. If 't1 == t2' is true, both variables point to the exact same memory location (the same object), so 't1.equals(t2)' must return true.

questions.text: Given two reference variables t1 and t2, if t1.equals(t2) is true, t1 == t2 ___________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: is always true

choices.label: B
choices.text: is always false

choices.label: C
choices.text: may be true or false

questions.explanation: The 'equals' method checks for logical equality (content), while '==' checks for reference equality (memory location). Two objects, such as two different String instances containing the same text, can be logically equal ('equals' is true) but reside at different memory addresses ('==' is false).

questions.text:
Analyze the following code. Please select all that apply.

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("Beijing");
list.add("Tokyo");
list.add("Shanghai");
list.set(3, "Hong Kong");

choices.label: A
choices.text: The last line in the code causes a runtime error because there is no element at index 3 in the array list.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The last line in the code has a compile error because there is no element at index 3 in the array list.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If you replace the last line by list.add(3, "Hong Kong"), the code will compile and run fine.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If you replace the last line by list.add(4, "Hong Kong"), the code will compile and run fine.

questions.explanation: The 'set(index, element)' method replaces an existing element and requires the index to be within the current bounds (0 to size-1). Since the size is 3, index 3 is out of bounds, causing a runtime error. However, the 'add(index, element)' method allows inserting an element at the end of the list (at index equal to 'size'), so 'list.add(3, ...)' is valid while 'list.add(4, ...)' is not.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

ArrayList<java.util.Date> list = new ArrayList<>();
java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();   
list.add(d);
list.add(d);    
System.out.println((list.get(0) == list.get(1)) + " " 
  + (list.get(0)).equals(list.get(1)));

choices.label: A
choices.text: true false

choices.label: B
choices.text: false true

choices.label: C
choices.text: true true

choices.label: D
choices.text: false false

questions.explanation: The code creates one Date object 'd' and adds that same reference to the list twice. Since both indices 0 and 1 in the list store the exact same reference, 'list.get(0) == list.get(1)' is true. Consequently, 'equals' also returns true because the object is compared to itself.

questions.text:
What is the output of the following code?

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
String s1 = new String("Java");   
String s2 = new String("Java");   
list.add(s1);
list.add(s2);    
System.out.println((list.get(0) == list.get(1)) + " " 
  + (list.get(0)).equals(list.get(1)));

choices.label: A
choices.text: true false

choices.label: B
choices.text: false true

choices.label: C
choices.text: true true

choices.label: D
choices.text: false false

questions.explanation: Two separate String objects are created using 'new', meaning 's1' and 's2' have different memory addresses. When they are added to the list, the list stores these different references. Therefore, 'list.get(0) == list.get(1)' is false. However, 'equals' returns true because the content of both strings is identical.

questions.text: You can create an ArrayList using _________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: new ArrayList[]

choices.label: B
choices.text: new ArrayList[100]

choices.label: C
choices.text: new ArrayList<>()

choices.label: D
choices.text: ArrayList()

questions.explanation: To create an ArrayList, you use the 'new' keyword followed by the constructor 'ArrayList<>()'. The diamond operator '<>' is used for type inference, and the parentheses are required to call the constructor. Options A and B incorrectly use array initialization syntax, and Option D is missing the 'new' keyword.

questions.text: Invoking _________ removes all elements in an ArrayList x.

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.remove()

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.clean()

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.delete()

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.empty()

choices.label: E
choices.text: x.clear()

questions.explanation: The 'clear()' method is a standard method in the Java ArrayList class used to remove all elements from the list, effectively setting its size to zero. Other options like clean(), delete(), or empty() do not exist in the ArrayList API.

questions.text: Suppose ArrayList x contains two strings [Beijing, Singapore]. Which of the following methods will cause the list to become [Beijing, Chicago, Singapore]?

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.add("Chicago")

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.add(0, "Chicago")

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.add(1, "Chicago")

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.add(2, "Chicago")

questions.explanation: ArrayList indexing is zero-based. To insert 'Chicago' between 'Beijing' (index 0) and 'Singapore' (index 1), you use the 'add(index, element)' method with an index of 1. This places 'Chicago' at index 1 and shifts 'Singapore' to index 2.

questions.text: Suppose ArrayList x contains two strings [Beijing, Singapore]. Which of the following method will cause the list to become [Beijing]? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.remove("Singapore")

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.remove(0)

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.remove(1)

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.remove(2)

questions.explanation: To remove 'Singapore' from the list [Beijing, Singapore], you can either remove by object reference using x.remove("Singapore") or remove by index. Since 'Singapore' is the second element, its index is 1, making x.remove(1) also correct.

questions.text: Suppose ArrayList x contains two strings [Beijing, Singapore]. Which of the following method will cause runtime errors? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.get(1)

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.set(2, "New York");

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.get(2)

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.remove(2)

choices.label: E
choices.text: x.size()

questions.explanation: The list [Beijing, Singapore] has a size of 2, meaning valid indices are 0 and 1. Accessing index 2 through set(), get(), or remove() will throw an IndexOutOfBoundsException. x.get(1) and x.size() are valid operations as they reference an existing index and the list properties respectively.

questions.text: Invoking _________ returns the first element in an ArrayList x.

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.first()

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.get(0)

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.get(1)

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.get()

questions.explanation: In Java, ArrayLists use zero-based indexing. Therefore, the first element is always located at index 0 and is accessed using the 'get(0)' method. 'first()' and 'get()' without arguments are not valid methods for an ArrayList.

questions.text: Invoking _________ returns the number of the elements in an ArrayList x.

choices.label: A
choices.text: x.getSize()

choices.label: B
choices.text: x.getLength(0)

choices.label: C
choices.text: x.length(1)

choices.label: D
choices.text: x.size()

questions.explanation: The 'size()' method is the standard way to retrieve the number of elements currently stored in an ArrayList. Unlike arrays which use a '.length' property, or strings which use a 'length()' method, ArrayLists specifically use 'size()'.

questions.text:
Suppose an ArrayList list contains {"red", "green", "red", "green"}. What is the list after the following code?

list.remove("red");

choices.label: A
choices.text: {"red", "green", "red", "green"}

choices.label: B
choices.text: {"green", "red", "green"}

choices.label: C
choices.text: {"green", "green"}

choices.label: D
choices.text: {"red", "green", "green"}

questions.explanation: The 'remove(Object o)' method in the ArrayList class only removes the first occurrence of the specified element. In the list {"red", "green", "red", "green"}, only the first "red" at index 0 is removed, leaving {"green", "red", "green"}.

questions.text:
Suppose an ArrayList list contains {"red", "red", "green"}. What is the list after the following code?

String element = "red";
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)
  if (list.get(i).equals(element))
    list.remove(element);

choices.label: A
choices.text: {"red", "red", "green"}

choices.label: B
choices.text: {"red", "green"}

choices.label: C
choices.text: {"green"}

questions.explanation: When you remove an element from an ArrayList during a forward loop, the remaining elements shift left. After removing the first 'red' at index 0, the second 'red' shifts to index 0. Because the loop counter 'i' increments to 1, it skips the second 'red' and moves to 'green', leaving {"red", "green"}.

questions.text:
Suppose an ArrayList list contains {"red", "red", "green"}. What is the list after the following code?

String element = "red";
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)
  if (list.get(i).equals(element)) {
    list.remove(element);
    i--;
  }

choices.label: A
choices.text: {"red", "red", "green"}

choices.label: B
choices.text: {"red", "green"}

choices.label: C
choices.text: {"green"}

questions.explanation: This code correctly removes all occurrences of 'red' because it decrements the loop counter 'i' after a removal. When an element is removed and the list shifts, 'i--' ensures that the next iteration re-checks the current index, capturing the element that just shifted into that position.

questions.text:
Suppose an ArrayList list contains {"red", "red", "green"}. What is the list after the following code?

String element = "red";
for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
  if (list.get(i).equals(element))
    list.remove(element);

choices.label: A
choices.text: {"red", "red", "green"}

choices.label: B
choices.text: {"red", "green"}

choices.label: C
choices.text: {"green"}

questions.explanation: The loop iterates backwards from the end of the list. When the condition matches, 'list.remove(element)' is called. This method removes the first occurrence of the specified element found in the list. On the first iteration where a match is found (index 1), the 'red' at index 0 is removed. In the next iteration (index 0), the remaining 'red' (now at index 0) is removed, leaving only 'green'.

questions.text:
The output from the following code is __________.

java.util.ArrayList<String> list 
  = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
list.add("New York"); 
java.util.ArrayList<String> list1 = list;
list.add("Atlanta"); 
list1.add("Dallas"); 
System.out.println(list1);

choices.label: A
choices.text: [New York]

choices.label: B
choices.text: [New York, Atlanta]

choices.label: C
choices.text: [New York, Atlanta, Dallas]

choices.label: D
choices.text: [New York, Dallas]

questions.explanation: In Java, assigning one object variable to another (list1 = list) copies the reference, meaning both variables point to the same object in memory. Modifications made through either variable affect the same ArrayList. Thus, all three elements ('New York', 'Atlanta', and 'Dallas') are added to the single shared list.

questions.text:
Show the output of the following code:

String[] array = {"red", "green", "blue"};
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(array));
list.add(0, "red");
System.out.println(list);

choices.label: A
choices.text: ["red", "green", "blue", "red"]

choices.label: B
choices.text: ["red", "green", "blue"]

choices.label: C
choices.text: ["red", "red", "green", "blue"]

choices.label: D
choices.text: ["red", "green", "red", "blue"]

questions.explanation: The code initializes a list with ['red', 'green', 'blue']. The method 'list.add(0, "red")' inserts the string 'red' at the specified index (0) and shifts the existing elements to the right, resulting in ['red', 'red', 'green', 'blue'].

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

Double[] array = {1, 2, 3};
ArrayList<Double> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(array));
System.out.println(list);

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [1, 2, 3].

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [1.0, 2.0, 3.0].

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code has a compile error because an integer such as 1 is automatically converted into an Integer object, but the array element type is Double.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code has a compile error because asList(array) requires that the array elements are objects.

questions.explanation: Java's auto-boxing does not support two-step conversions for array initialization. While an 'int' literal can be boxed to an 'Integer', it cannot be boxed directly to a 'Double'. Since 'Double' is the required type for the array elements, the 'int' literals 1, 2, and 3 cause a compilation error.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

double[] array = {1, 2, 3};
ArrayList<Double> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(array));
System.out.println(list);

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [1, 2, 3].

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [1.0, 2.0, 3.0].

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code has a compile error because an integer such as 1 is automatically converted into an Integer object, but the array element type is Double.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code has a compile error because asList(array) requires that the array elements are objects.

questions.explanation: The 'Arrays.asList()' method does not auto-box primitive arrays (like 'double[]') into lists of their wrapper types (like 'List<Double>'). Instead, it treats the entire primitive array as a single object, creating a 'List<double[]>'. This list is incompatible with the 'ArrayList<Double>' constructor, leading to a compile error.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

double[] c = {1, 2, 3};
System.out.println(java.util.Collections.max(c));

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code is correct and displays 3.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code is correct and displays 3.0.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code has a compile error on Collections.max(c). c cannot be an array.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code has a compile error on double[] c = {1, 2, 3}.

questions.explanation: The 'java.util.Collections.max()' method is designed to work with objects that implement the 'Collection' interface. Because 'c' is a primitive array ('double[]') and not a collection, the code results in a compilation error.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

Integer[] c = {3, 5};
java.util.Collections.shuffle(c);
System.out.println(java.util.Arrays.toString(c));

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [3, 5].

choices.label: B
choices.text: The code is correct and displays [5, 3].

choices.label: C
choices.text: The code has a compile error on Collections.shuffle(c). c cannot be an array.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code has a compile error on Integer[] c = {3, 5}.

questions.explanation: The 'java.util.Collections.shuffle()' method requires a 'List' as its argument. Since 'c' is an array ('Integer[]') rather than a 'List', the code fails to compile.

questions.text: A class design requires that a particular member variable must be accessible by any subclasses of this class, but otherwise not by classes which are not members of the same package. What should be done to achieve this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The variable should be marked public.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The variable should be marked private.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The variable should be marked protected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The variable should have no special access modifier.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The variable should be marked private and an accessor method provided.

questions.explanation: The 'protected' modifier allows a member to be accessed by any subclass (even in different packages) and by any other classes within the same package. This fulfills the requirement of being accessible to all subclasses while restricting general access from outside the package.

questions.text:
Which statements are most accurate regarding the following classes?

class A {
  private int i;
  protected int j;
}

class B extends A {
  private int k;
  protected int m;
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: An object of B contains data fields i, j, k, m.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An object of B contains data fields j, k, m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An object of B contains data fields j, m.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An object of B contains data fields k, m.

questions.explanation: When a class is instantiated, the object contains all instance variables defined in its class hierarchy, including those marked as 'private' in superclasses. While private fields from the superclass are not directly accessible by the subclass code, they still exist within the object's memory structure.

questions.text:
Which statements are most accurate regarding the following classes?

class A {
  private int i;
  protected int j;
}

class B extends A {
  private int k;
  protected int m;

  // some methods omitted
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the class B, an instance method can only access i, j, k, m.

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the class B, an instance method can only access j, k, m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In the class B, an instance method can only access j, m.

choices.label: D
choices.text: In the class B, an instance method can only access k, m.

questions.explanation: A subclass can access 'protected' and 'public' members of its superclass, as well as its own members (regardless of their modifier). However, it cannot access 'private' members of its superclass. Therefore, class B can access 'j' (protected from A), 'k' (private in B), and 'm' (protected in B), but not 'i' (private in A).

questions.text: Which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A public class can be accessed by a class from a different package.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A private method cannot be accessed by a class in a different package.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A protected method can be accessed by a subclass in a different package.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A method with no visibility modifier can be accessed by a class in a different package.

questions.explanation: Statement D is false because a method with no visibility modifier (also known as package-private or default access) can only be accessed by classes within the same package. It is not accessible to classes in a different package.

questions.text: What modifier should you use on a class so that a class in the same package can access it but a class (including a subclass) in a different package cannot access it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: public

choices.label: B
choices.text: private

choices.label: C
choices.text: protected

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the default modifier.

questions.explanation: The default modifier (no explicit modifier) provides package-level access. This ensures that only classes within the same package can access the class, while classes in other packages, including subclasses, cannot.

questions.text: What modifier should you use on the members of a class so that they are not accessible to another class in a different package, but are accessible to any subclasses in any package?

choices.label: A
choices.text: public

choices.label: B
choices.text: private

choices.label: C
choices.text: protected

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the default modifier.

questions.explanation: The 'protected' modifier allows class members to be accessed by classes within the same package and by subclasses located in any package. This distinguishes it from the default modifier, which would block access to subclasses in different packages.

questions.text: The visibility of these modifiers increases in this order:

choices.label: A
choices.text: private, protected, none (if no modifier is used), and public.

choices.label: B
choices.text: private, none (if no modifier is used), protected, and public.

choices.label: C
choices.text: none (if no modifier is used), private, protected, and public.

choices.label: D
choices.text: none (if no modifier is used), protected, private, and public.

questions.explanation: The levels of visibility in Java, from most restrictive to least restrictive, are: private (class only), none/default (package only), protected (package and subclasses), and public (everywhere).

questions.text: Which of the following classes cannot be extended?

choices.label: A
choices.text: class A {    }

choices.label: B
choices.text: class A {   private A() {  }}

choices.label: C
choices.text: final class A {    }

choices.label: D
choices.text: class A {   protected A() {  }}

questions.explanation: The 'final' keyword in a class declaration explicitly prevents that class from being extended (subclassed) by any other class.
